<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: command-contracts-and-semantic-interfaces
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/command-contracts-and-semantic-interfaces.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/command-contracts-and-semantic-interfaces.qmd@9f8589b
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Command Contracts and Semantic Interfaces

A command is where a request becomes inspectable.

The previous chapter followed intent as it moved toward execution. That trace
can name purpose, authority, artifacts, verification, feedback, and residuals,
but the stack still needs a smaller object at the point where planning begins.
It needs a command that is compact enough for machines to consume and explicit
enough for humans to audit.

That is the command contract. It is not a decorative prompt format. It is the
first semantic interface downstream layers are allowed to treat as planning
input. Natural language can remain attached, examples can help, style notes
can guide the result, and retrieved context can inform the work, but those
materials do not get to become hidden permission. The explicit fields control
dispatch.

The throughline is simple: language can inform work, but command fields govern
work.

## Problem

The stack needs a compact command language that is expressive enough for AI
cooperation and precise enough for validation.

Human requests arrive with more meaning than a schema can capture. They carry
tone, history, implied goals, examples, priorities, and sometimes ambiguity.
An AI system should not flatten all of that richness into sterile machine
syntax. But it also cannot let a blob of prose decide what tools may run, what
private information may be used, what output counts as success, or what should
happen when the work fails.

The command contract keeps the operational pieces visible. It names the role,
objective, context references, constraints, procedure, allowed means,
forbidden means, output contract, verification duties, failure behavior,
authority ceiling, approval requirements, expected artifacts, feedback route,
re-contract points, dispatch blockers, and non-claims.

Software Magic Grimoire supplies the vocabulary for bounded operative
language: roles, objectives, constraints, procedures, outputs, verification,
failure behavior, guards, loops, handoffs, and recovery paths. VIEA supplies
the intent-to-execution spine that makes those fields part of a larger work
trace. GenesisCode adds the discipline that privileged effects need
recognizable protocol shapes, provenance, obligations, and capability
boundaries. Cognitive Compilation and Talos show why the command must lower
cleanly into source plans, semantic atoms, typed jobs, evidence records,
approval gates, and replayable work.

The command contract is therefore the earliest place where helpfulness has to
become a bounded obligation.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Prompt prose often hides objectives, constraints, output contracts, and
failure behavior in unstructured language.

A free-form prompt can be excellent for exploration. It lets a human think in
ordinary language and lets a model infer structure. But exploration and
execution are different states. The moment a prompt becomes a work order, its
implicit parts start to matter: what is the actual objective, what sources are
allowed, what constraints outrank convenience, what artifact should exist at
the end, what verifier should judge it, what side effects are permitted, and
what should happen if the instruction conflicts with context?

Without a semantic interface, the system guesses. It may treat an example as a
requirement, a style note as a constraint, a retrieved passage as an
instruction, or a likely tool path as permission. It may also produce a good
answer that cannot be accepted because no one recorded what acceptance meant.

Structured prompts can fail in the same way. Moving vague prose into YAML or
JSON does not make the command governable. A field that says "do what is
needed" is still unbounded. An output contract that says "make it good" is
not testable. An authority field inferred from context is not authorization.

Dafny provides a useful outside comparison from specification-oriented
programming. Its lesson for this chapter is not that the ASI Stack has Dafny
proofs for command contracts. It does not. The useful comparison is the
separation between a specification, an implementation, a verifier, and runtime
behavior. Command contracts borrow that separation at the human/AI boundary:
make the obligations explicit before pretending they can be checked.

## Core Claim

Command contracts should use explicit semantic interfaces for objective,
context, constraints, procedure, output, verification, and failure behavior.

The live AI/research chapter records this as a design rationale at `argument`
support. The repository has source notes, schema surfaces, fixture validation,
finite Lean predicates, and a synthetic plan-execution harness. Those artifacts
support record discipline and cross-record consistency. They do not prove
prompt safety, parser completeness, semantic extraction quality, dispatch
enforcement, prompt-injection resistance, empirical productivity gains, or
deployed execution.

The claim is deliberately modest: if a command is going to become planning
input, it should expose the fields that downstream layers need in order to
plan, block, clarify, verify, refuse, or record a residual.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a semantic firewall.

The raw request, conversation history, retrieved context, examples, and style
instructions can all sit near the command. They can inform interpretation. But
they cannot silently override explicit objective, constraints, authority,
output, verification, or failure behavior.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Request["Natural-language request"] --> Extract["Semantic extraction"]
  Context["Context, examples, prior text"] --> Review["Precedence review"]
  Extract --> Fields["Command fields"]
  Fields --> Review
  Review -->|passes| Planning["Planning input"]
  Review -->|missing field| Clarify["Clarify or re-contract"]
  Review -->|conflict| Residual["Conflict residual"]
  Review -->|inferred authority| Block["Dispatch blocked"]
  Clarify --> Fields
  Residual --> Ledger["Evidence and residual ledger"]
  Block --> Ledger
```

The contract has two jobs. First, it compresses the work into fields another
layer can inspect. Second, it records the confidence of those fields. A
confirmed field is different from a defaulted field. A policy-imposed
constraint is different from a source-derived requirement. An inferred tool
path is different from an authorized tool path. Missing values are not
annoyances; they are dispatch blockers or re-contract points.

Command validation states keep this pre-execution boundary explicit. A `draft`
contract is still being extracted. `field_complete` means the slots exist, not
that they are trustworthy. `conflict_detected` means context or fields clash
with explicit constraints. `authority_inferred` means a means, tool,
disclosure, publication, or other effect was guessed rather than granted.
`dispatch_blocked` means a required output, verification, failure, approval,
or authority field is missing or vague. Only `validated_for_planning` can
lower into a plan graph, and even that state does not say the plan or final
artifact is correct.

This is why the command interface belongs between intent and planning. It
lets the planner consume bounded work rather than ambient desire.

## Interfaces

Command semantics are carried by the Command Contract.

The minimum interface includes:

- `contract_id`
- `intent_id`
- `validation_state`
- `role`
- `objective`
- `context_refs`
- `field_provenance`
- `field_confidence`
- `constraints`
- `procedure`
- `allowed_means`
- `forbidden_means`
- `bounded_defaults`
- `output_contract`
- `verification`
- `failure_behavior`
- `authority_ceiling`
- `authority_basis`
- `required_approvals`
- `expected_artifacts`
- `feedback_route`
- `recontract_points`
- `dispatch_blockers`
- `non_claims`

Planning consumes the command. Execution enforces allowed means and output
contracts. Verification checks declared criteria and failure behavior. Evidence
records which fields were satisfied, repaired, escalated, superseded, or left
residual.

The important interface detail is field status. `confirmed` fields may support
planning because they were explicitly stated or accepted. `policy_imposed`
fields can constrain planning but cannot broaden authority. `source_derived`
fields can carry source-limited requirements. `defaulted` fields need a scope
and a re-contract trigger. `inferred` fields can help draft a command, but
they cannot authorize side effects. `missing` fields block dispatch or narrow
the response to clarification.

A command can therefore be useful before it is executable. That distinction is
one of its main safety properties.

## Invariants

A command exposes output and verification requirements.

Failure behavior is declared.

Implicit instructions do not override explicit constraints.

Field provenance and confidence remain visible to planning and verification.

Inferred or defaulted authority cannot authorize side effects.

The override invariant is the center of the chapter. If a retrieved passage,
tool result, prior conversation, or user-provided example conflicts with the
command contract, the system should not choose the most fluent interpretation.
It should record the conflict, ask, narrow, block dispatch, or create a
residual.

The output-test coupling matters too. A command without an expected artifact
or verifier is not ready for governed planning because later layers cannot
tell whether the work succeeded.

## Failure modes

Semantic ambiguity produces false agreement. The human, model, planner, and
reviewer all think the command is clear until the artifact arrives and exposes
that they meant different things.

Prompt injection through context attacks the semantic firewall. A retrieved
or pasted instruction tries to act like a higher-priority command. The contract
must make that visible as conflict, not as ordinary context.

Unspecified output contracts make verification subjective. If success is not
named, the system may optimize for fluency instead of acceptance.

Field laundering happens when vague prose is moved into a formal slot and
treated as if it became testable. A field is not governable merely because it
has a key name.

Authority inference is the dangerous case. The system sees a likely means and
treats it as if the human granted it. In a governed stack, inferred means can
support a draft or a clarification question. They cannot open a side-effect
path.

Trace laundering is a related evidence failure. A valid command schema
instance is not execution, and a synthetic fixture is not a deployed command
firewall.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is a public-safe `command_contract` record
plus fixture checks that distinguish valid command shape from blocked command
state.

The current repository already has the schema surface, protocol fixture
validation, finite Lean gates for required fields and explicit-constraint
precedence, and the synthetic plan-execution harness. Those checks are useful
because they make command fields, failure behavior, dispatch blockers,
approval state, and cross-record consistency inspectable.

The next small implementation slice should add a command-conflict fixture set:
one clean command, one missing output contract, one hidden-context override,
one inferred-authority case, and one declared failure-behavior case. That
would test the semantic firewall more directly while still staying below any
claim of deployed prompt security.

The current MVI remains protocol discipline, not behavior proof.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a semantic control language for governed work.

In that endpoint, command contracts are not prompt templates. They are the
shared language used by humans, planners, verifiers, job runners, policy
layers, and future AI agents to agree on what the next unit of work means
before any execution path opens.

Every command field carries provenance, confidence, precedence, and dispatch
consequence. Context can be rich without becoming authority. Examples can
guide output without rewriting constraints. Style can help communication
without changing verification. Source material can inform the answer without
turning into a hidden instruction channel.

The product-level version would include reusable command templates, conflict
checkers, field-confidence policies, verifier-ready obligations, lowering
rules into plan graphs, and dispatch gates that refuse vague, conflicting,
missing, or authority-inferred commands. It would make "no" and "not yet" as
normal as "continue."

That endpoint is still architecture. It needs parser behavior, conflict tests,
override tests, dispatch enforcement, downstream traces, and human review
before it can support stronger claims.

## Summary

Command contracts turn language into inspectable obligation.

They keep the raw richness of human communication nearby while exposing the
fields that planning, execution, verification, and evidence need. Their value
is not bureaucracy. Their value is precedence: explicit objective, constraint,
authority, output, verification, and failure behavior outrank helpful but
unbounded context.

The command surface should be short enough to use and strict enough to reject.
That is how a governed stack keeps language useful without letting it become
ambient control.

## Handoff

Once the command exposes objective, constraints, output contract,
verification, failure behavior, and authority boundaries, the stack can ask
what order the work should happen in. Planning as a Control Layer takes the
validated command surface and turns it into dependencies, budgets, context
requests, authority limits, blocked-node records, and stop conditions before
any executable job exists.
